28: ten ekklesian tou theou hen peripoiesato dia tou haimatos tou idiou (tēn ekklēsian tou theou hēn peripoiesato dia tou haimatos tou idiou): arguably the most theologically dense sentence in Acts. Ekklesia tou theou is Paul's normal Corinthian usage. Peripoieomai (acquire for oneself) echoes LXX Isa 43:21 and Exod 19:5 where YHWH acquires Israel as his special possession. Critically: dia tou haimatos tou idiou — 'through his own blood'. Idios (own/personal) can modify the understood subject (God, whose Son's blood is 'his own') or be read as 'the blood of the one who is his own [Son]'. Either reading makes this the most explicit Acts Christology: God-who-owns-the-blood, or blood that belongs to God himself. The elders of Ephesus are charged to shepherd what God purchased at this cost.
1: metapempsAmenos tous mathetas (metapempsamenos tous mathētas): after the Ephesian riot subsides, Paul gathers the disciples and departs for Macedonia. Metapempsamenos (having summoned) shows pastoral deliberateness — Paul does not flee but formally commissions and greets the community before moving on. The Macedonian visit and return south through Greece occupies vv. 1-3 in summary travel notation.
2: parakalesAs autous logo pollo (parakalesas autous logō pollō): 'having encouraged them with much speech'. Parakalō is the pastoral strengthening-word that runs through Acts (14:22; 15:32; 18:27); logō pollō implies extended personal teaching rather than a brief farewell. Paul's ministry pattern across Macedonia was sustained instruction before departure — building communities able to stand after he leaves.
3: genomenou epiboules auto hypo ton Ioudaion (genomenēs epiboulēs autō hypo tōn Ioudaiōn): a plot against Paul by Jews in Corinth forces a route change northward instead of sailing directly to Syria. This is the fourth such plot in Acts (9:24; 20:3; 23:12-16; 25:3); the pattern of Jewish hostility to Paul's mission traces a consistent arc. The overruling of the plot by divine providence is implicit in genomenēs — it arose and was discovered before it succeeded.
4: Sopatros Pyrrhou Berraios: the delegation list of Paul's travel companions reads as a roll-call of the churches represented in the collection for Jerusalem (2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28). Sopater from Beroea, Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from Asia — each representative carries his church's gift. The journey to Jerusalem is thus a concrete embodiment of Gentile-church solidarity with Jewish believers.
5: houtoi proelthontes emeinan hemas en Troadi: the third-person shifts back to first at v.5 (the 'we' resumes after 16:17), placing the narrator back in the travel party. Troas was the site of Paul's Macedonian vision (16:8-9) — the gateway to Europe. The group reconvenes there, creating narrative symmetry: what began in Troas (European mission) is reviewed and completed there.
6: meta tas hemas ton azymOn (meta tas hēmeras tōn azymōn): 'after the Days of Unleavened Bread'. Luke marks the Passover-season calendar in a way that gives the Troas gathering a paschal frame — the breaking of bread in v.7 during this season carries eucharistic weight heightened by Passover's sacrificial context. The deliberate calendrical note is one of several Lukan liturgical markers.
7: en te mia ton sabbaton... tou klasai arton (en tē mia tōn sabbatōn... tou klasai arton): 'on the first day of the week... to break bread'. This is the earliest NT evidence for Sunday as the assembly day (1 Cor 16:2; Rev 1:10 confirm the pattern). Klasai arton carries the eucharistic resonance established in Luke 22:19 and 24:35. Paul's extended teaching — lasting until midnight — reflects the synagogue-then-meal pattern adapted to Sunday assemblies.
8: lampades hikanai en to hyperoo (lampades hikanai en tō hyperōō): 'many lamps in the upper room'. Luke's realistic detail — the room crowded, hot, poorly ventilated with oil-lamp smoke — sets up the Eutychus episode naturalistically. The hyperōon (upper story room) is the same word for the Jerusalem upper room of Acts 1:13; the gathering in Troas is structurally continuous with the original community.
9: Eutycho... katapheromenos hypno bathy (Eutychō... katapheromenos hypnō bathei): 'Eutychus... overcome by deep sleep'. Eutychus means 'fortunate' — ironic given his fall. Katapherō (overwhelm/weigh down) with hypnō bathei (deep sleep) conveys total incapacitation. The detail of his sitting in the window suggests he sought air; the three-story fall (apo tou tristegou) was fatal. Luke is careful: ērthē nekros — 'he was taken up dead'.
10: epipesoN auto kai symperilaboN eipen (epipesōn autō kai symperiabōn eipen): Paul's action echoes Elijah falling on the child of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:21) and Elisha stretching over the Shunammite's son (2 Kgs 4:34). Symperilambanō (embrace/enfold) is a hapax — the physical embrace conveys the life-restoration. Mē thorubeisthe, he psyche autou en autō estin: 'do not be troubled, his life is in him' — present tense, the miracle already complete before the crowd is reassured.
11: klasas ton arton kai geusamenos (klasas ton arton kai geusamenos): the miracle-interruption resolved, Paul returns to the eucharistic meal. The narrative sequence — eucharist disrupted by death, life restored, eucharist resumed — enacts the theology: the Lord's Supper is the ongoing celebration of life over death. HomilesAs epi poly (having spoken at length) — Paul preaches until dawn, an all-night catechetical vigil.
12: egagon de ton paida zonta (ēgagon de ton paida zōnta): 'they brought the young man away alive'. ZōnTa (living) echoes the resurrection vocabulary throughout Luke-Acts (zōn: Acts 1:3, 25:19). The community is 'not-a-little comforted' (pareклēthēsan ou metriōs) — litotes for overwhelming relief and strengthened faith. The Troas community receives the same life-over-death sign Paul proclaimed in word.
13: hēmeis de proelthontes epi to ploion anechthēmen epi ten Asson: the travel narrative resumes with 'we', Paul walking overland alone to Assos while the others sail. The deliberate separation — Paul's choice to walk — reflects the pastorally reflective rhythm that characterizes his ministry: theological instruction at night, solitary travel by day. The coastal geography (Assos, Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus) is precisely observed.
14: synballoN hēmin eis ten Asson... parelabomen auton: Paul joining the ship at Assos. Symballō (join/meet) is a nautical-travel term. The rapid place-list of vv. 14-15 (Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus) reflects a voyage hugging the Aegean coast. Luke's precision is consistent with the 'we' source's navigational detail — the narrator was present.
15: te de hestere erchomenoi anteikrys Chiou: passage past Chios — the island of Homer's reputed birthplace. Luke's geographic notation in the sea-journey sections is the most precise in the NT; the Miletus decision (v.16) to bypass Ephesus for time explains the coastal route taken.
16: kekrikei gar ho Paulos parapleusei ten Epheson (kekrikei gar ho Paulos parapleusei tēn Epheson): 'Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus'. Kekrikei (had decided/judged) is a perfect-pluperfect showing a prior firm decision. The reason: hopōs mē genētai autō chronotribēsai en tē Asia — 'so he would not lose time in Asia'. Paul's urgency to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost (the same pilgrimage-feast as Acts 2) drives the route. Yet he still calls the Ephesian elders to Miletus — pastoral obligation and eschatological urgency coexist.
17: apo de tes Miletou pempsas eis Epheson metekalesato tous presbyterous tes ekklesias: Paul's summons of the Ephesian elders to Miletus initiates the most sustained pastoral speech in Acts (vv.18-35). Presbyteroi (elders) here are interchangeable with episkopoi (overseers, v.28), establishing NT precedent for the same office designated by both terms. The Miletus address is Paul's only speech to Christians in Acts; all others address Jews or pagans.
18: pos meth hymon ton panta chronon egenomen (pōs meth' hymōn ton panta chronon egenomēn): 'how I was with you the whole time'. The Miletus speech opens with an autobiographical defense-and-testament that echoes the farewell discourses of Moses (Deut 29-31), Samuel (1 Sam 12), and Jesus (John 13-17). Paul's three-year Ephesian ministry (v.31) is summoned as evidence of integrity: his conduct was visible to all.
19: douleuon to kyrio meta pases tapeinophrosynes (douleuōn tō kyriō meta pasēs tapeinophrosynēs): 'serving the Lord with all humility'. Tapeinophrosynē (humility/lowliness of mind) is virtually a Pauline coinage (Phil 2:3; Eph 4:2; Col 2:18, 23; 3:12) — it occurs rarely before Paul and frequently after him. The tears (dakryōn) and trials from Jewish plots (peirasmōn tōn symbantōn moi en tais epiboulais tōn Ioudaiōn) authenticate the pastoral biography.
20: hōs ouden hypesteilamēn ton sympheron (hōs ouden hypesteilamēn tōn sympherontōn): 'I held nothing back of what was profitable'. Hypostellō (shrink back/withhold) is a strong word — to conceal or suppress what ought to be declared. Paul's claim is total disclosure: no withheld teaching, no pastorally convenient omissions. didaskon dēmosia kai kat oikous — public teaching in the synagogue/agora and private house-church instruction — reflect the dual educational venues of Pauline mission.
21: tēn eis theon metanoian kai pistin eis ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun: 'repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus' — the most compact summary of Pauline-Lukan gospel content in Acts. The double movement — eis theon (toward God) and eis ton kyrion (toward the Lord) — is not redundant: repentance is the reorientation toward God as sovereign; faith is the trust in Jesus as mediator. Both Jews and Greeks receive the same message, establishing its universality.
22: deo to pneumati poreuomai (deō tō pneumati poreuomai, 'bound in the spirit I go'): the ambiguity of pneuma here (divine Spirit or Paul's own spirit) is probably deliberate — Paul's own resolve is the vehicle of the Spirit's compulsion. The Jerusalem journey is framed as a Spirit-driven necessity (dei) that mirrors Jesus's own Jerusalem journey in Luke 9:51. Ta en autē synantēsonta moi mē eidōs: 'not knowing what will happen to me there' — Paul's ignorance of specifics, not of outcome (v.23 confirms suffering awaits).
23: to pneuma to hagion kata polin diamartyretai (to pneuma to hagion kata polin diamartyretai): 'the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city'. Diamartyromai (solemnly testify/warn) is the Spirit's prophetic voice in every community Paul visits. The consistent content: desma kai thlipseis me menousin — 'bonds and afflictions await me'. The Spirit does not warn Paul away from Jerusalem; he forewarns him of what obedience costs. This distincts Paul's prophetic awareness from the disciples' protective dissuasion (v.4; 21:12).
24: all oud logon poioumai ten psychen mou timian emaut (all' oud' logon poioumai tēn psychēn mou timian emautō): 'I consider my life of no value to myself'. Logon poioumai (reckon/make account of) is accounting language — Paul does not count his life an asset worth protecting at the expense of mission. dromos (dromos): the foot-race or chariot-course; Paul's ministry as a race to be finished appears in 2 Tim 4:7 and Phil 3:12-14. ten diakonian: ministry-as-service, the characteristic NT inversion of Greco-Roman status. diamartyrasthai to euangelion tes charitos tou theou: 'to testify fully to the gospel of the grace of God' — charis as the content that defines the message.
25: ouketi opsesthe to prosōpon mou (ouketi opsesthe to prosōpon mou): 'you will see my face no longer'. This farewell declaration is the rhetorical pivot of the speech — the eschatological imminence that makes the charge in vv.28-31 urgent. Paul's knowledge that the Ephesian elders will not see him again frames the remaining instruction as a testament. The solemn declaration of completion — diēlthon kēryssōn ten basileian (I went about preaching the kingdom) — marks the end of this chapter of mission.
26: katharos eimI apo tou haimatos pantōn (katharos eimi apo tou haimatos pantōn): 'I am clean of the blood of all'. The metaphor echoes Ezek 33:1-9 where the watchman who fails to warn the wicked is guilty of the blood of those who perish. Paul's three-year ministry of total disclosure (v.27: pasan ten boulen tou theou) absolves him of pastoral responsibility for any who turn away. The language is forensic — Paul has been a faithful watchman; his hands are clean.
27: pasan ten boulen tou theou (pasan tēn boulēn tou theou): 'the whole counsel/purpose of God'. Boulē (plan/will/counsel) is Luke's characteristic word for God's sovereign purpose unfolding through history (Luke 7:30; Acts 2:23; 4:28; 13:36; 20:27). The claim to have proclaimed 'all' of it is the claim of a complete apostolic commission faithfully discharged — nothing withheld or softened. This verse grounds the integrity of Pauline teaching as full-canonical, not selective.
29: eiseleusontai meta ten aphixin mou lykoi bareis eis hymas (eiseleusontai meta tēn aphixin mou lykoi bareis eis hymas): 'after my departure, savage wolves will come in among you'. Lykoi bareis (savage/heavy wolves) — the same pastoral metaphor Jesus used in Matt 7:15 for false prophets. Paul foresees two threats: external wolves (v.29) and internal distorters (v.30: ex hymōn autōn, 'from among yourselves'). The prediction proved accurate: the Ephesian letters (Ephesians, 1-2 Timothy, Revelation 2:1-7) all address precisely these threats.
30: ex hymōn autōn anastēsontai andres lalountes diestramena (ex hymōn autōn anastēsontai andres lalountes diestramMena): 'from among yourselves men will arise speaking perverse things'. Diestrammena (twisted/perverted) — truth bent out of shape, not openly denied but distorted. Apospān tous mathētas (to draw away the disciples) — the aim is not just error but faction. Paul's farewell foresees the Ephesian crisis that 1-2 Timothy address: teachers within the community who use their position to recruit followers to themselves.
31: dio grēgoreite, mnēmoneuontes hoti trietian nychta kai hēmeran ouk epausamēn: 'therefore be alert, remembering that for three years night and day I did not stop'. Grēgoreō (keep watch) is the eschatological watchfulness-word (Mark 13:35; Rev 3:2-3). Three years of pastoral presence — the longest Paul stayed anywhere except Corinth (18 months) — constitutes the model that the elders are to reproduce. Mnēmoneuontes (remembering) makes Paul's ministry an ongoing standard against which future teaching is measured.
32: to rhēma tes charitos autou to dynamenon oikodomesai kai dounai ten klēronomiAn (tō rhēmati tēs charitos autou tō dynamenō oikodomēsai kai dounai tēn klēronomiān): 'the word of his grace, which is able to build up and give the inheritance'. Paul commends the Ephesian elders not to their own competence but to the word as the agent of construction. Oikodomeō (build up/edify) and klēronomia (inheritance) together frame the church as a building-under-construction and a community with an eschatological destination. The phrase 'tois hēgiasmenois pasin' (all who are sanctified) grounds the inheritance in God's prior sanctifying work.
33: argyriou e chrysiou e himatismou oudenos epethymēsa: 'I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing'. The three categories — money and clothing — cover the standard forms of pastoral exploitation in antiquity. Paul's disclaimer echoes Samuel's farewell in 1 Sam 12:3 ('I have taken no one's ox or donkey, nor have I cheated or oppressed anyone'). The pastoral-integrity defence was a stock form in philosophical and rhetorical tradition; Paul fills it with genuine autobiographical content.
34: autoi ginōskete hoti tais chreiais mou kai tois ousin met emou hyperetēsan hai cheires autai: 'you yourselves know that these hands provided for my needs and for those who were with me'. Hai cheires autai (these very hands) — the physical demonstration of self-support. Paul's tent-making is mentioned in Acts 18:3; the Corinthian letters show he made this self-support policy a deliberate mark of apostolic integrity distinguishing him from fee-taking sophists and wonder-workers.
35: dei antilambAnesthai ton asthenounton, mnēmoneuein te ton logon tou kyriou Iesou hoti autos eipen, Makarion estin mallon didonai e lambanein: 'you must help the weak, remembering the words of the Lord Jesus that he himself said, It is more blessed to give than to receive'. This saying of Jesus (rhēma tou kyriou Iēsou) is not found in any gospel — the only example of a Jesus-saying in Acts that is not in the canonical gospels but is attributed to Jesus by the earliest witness. Makarion estin didonai: the beatitude-form applied to generosity is continuous with Jesus's Sermon-on-the-Mount ethic.
36: kai tauta eipOn, theis ta gonata autou syn pasin autois proseuxato: 'having said these things, he knelt down with them all and prayed'. Theis ta gonata (kneeling) is the posture of intense intercession (Acts 7:60; 21:5; Eph 3:14). The prayer concludes the Miletus address without being recorded — Luke preserves the gesture, not the content, as the seal of the pastoral encounter. The corporate kneeling enacts the unity of purpose the address called for.
37: hikanos de klauthmos egeneto pantōn: 'there was much weeping from all'. The farewell scene — tears, embraces, kisses, grief at the finality — is the most emotionally detailed passage in Acts. Epipiptō (fall upon) for the embraces echoes the father in the parable (Luke 15:20); katephiloun (kissed repeatedly, imperfect) conveys sustained farewell-affection. The community Paul built loves him as a father.
38: odynomenoi malista epi to logō hō eirēken hoti ouketi mellouseN to prosōpon autou theōrein: 'grieving especially over the word he had spoken that they would see his face no longer'. The malista (especially) specifies what makes this parting unbearable: not the journey's danger but the finality of separation. Theōrein (behold) is Luke's word for attentive seeing; the elders will never again behold the pastor who gave them three years and the whole counsel of God. The grief is eschatologically realistic.
